Notice Of Motion And Motion To Withdraw Responses To Requests For Admissions; Memorandum Of Points & Authorities; Declaration Of Ilan N. Rosen Janfaza, Esq.; Exhibits
Off Calendar May-16-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: PLAINTIFFS FAYE HARRIS, AND STUART IVERSON'S Motion To Withdraw Responses To Requests For Admissions.

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Ilan N. Rosen Janfaza, Esq., Law Offices of, (310) 550-6000, for Plaintiff.
Henry P. Huff, Esq., of LAGASSE BRANCH BELL + KINKEAD LLP, (628) 222-5870, for Defendants.

The matter is not argued/contested. The court confirms that the tentative ruling was not appropriately contested, as specified in the tentative ruling. Therefore, oral argument will not be considered. The court's tentative ruling is adopted. Motion is denied. The Court takes judicial notice of prior court filings concerning Defendant's Requests for Admission to Plaintiffs. Plaintiffs first failed to oppose and to appear at the February 1, 2024 hearing which resulted in the admissions deemed admitted (against Plaintiffs) and monetary sanctions against Plaintiffs. Plaintiffs then moved for reconsideration but made no showing of "new, or different facts, circumstances, or law" as required by C.C.P. Sec. 1008 but claimed repeated calendaring errors. This Court found that Plaintiffs' claimed calendaring errors were not mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect as the repeated failure to calendar correctly constituted conduct falling below the professional standard of care and is therefore not excusable. Henderson v. Pacific Gas and Electric Co., 187 Cal. App. 4th 215 (2010). See also, County of San Bernardino v. Mancini et al., 83 Cal. App. 5th 1095 (2022). With the instant motion, Plaintiffs now argue, for the first time, that the oversight of Plaintiffs' counsel was due to illness. The fact that this claim was strangely omitted from the motion for reconsideration casts significant doubt on the veracity of this claim of illness. The claim of illness did not explain the repeated failure to comply with basic motion practice procedures. To the extent that this motion is one for discretionary relief based on a claim of attorney fault, Plaintiffs are not entitled to relief here as the repeated failure to calendar correctly and the curious omission of the illness from the motion for reconsideration, as presented in Plaintiffs' supporting declaration, again constituted conduct falling below the professional standard of care and is therefore not excusable. Plaintiffs additionally argue that they have "served verified, substantially code compliant responses" to Defendants' Requests for Admission and attached a copy of said responses as Exhibits 2 and 3 to the supporting declaration. However, the attached exhibits show that Plaintiffs' denials were only signed by counsel and not verified by the parties. As the Court of Appeal in St. Mary v. Superior Court, 223 Cal. App. 4th 762, (2014), recited: "unverified RFA response is not in substantial compliance with the law," this motion must be denied upon the moving papers as presented because Plaintiffs have not made any showing in three (3) consecutive motions that Defendants' motion to deem admissions admitted should not have been granted.

The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem at williamclynn@gmail.com.

Judge Pro Tem: William Lynn. Clerk: R. Woods. Not Reported. (302/JPT)

Court takes note that today's hearing was highly contentious and that the Court terminated the hearing due to moving party's counsel's disrespectful and flippant conduct during argument.

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling with minor modification as follows:

PLAINTIFFS FAYE HARRIS, STUART IVERSON'S Motion For Reconsideration Of The Court's Order Granting Defendants Udr, Inc., Channel Mission Bay, Lisa Quigley And Ethel Pacis'S Motion To Deem The Truth Of Matters Specified In Requests For Admission, Set One, Prop

Plaintiffs' motion is denied. Plaintiffs made no showing of "new, or different facts, circumstances, or law" as required by C.C.P. sec. 1008. To the extent that this motion is one for discretionary relief based on a claim of attorney fault similar to a C.C.P. sec. 473(b) motion, Plaintiffs are still not entitled to relief here as the repeated failure to calendar correctly, as presented in the moving party's supporting declaration, constituted conduct falling below the professional standard of care and is therefore not excusable. Henderson v. Pacific Gas and Electric Co, 187 Cal. App. 4th 215 (2010). See also, County of San Bernardino v. Mancini et al., 83 Cal. App. 5th 1095 (2022). Parties are also reminded to comply with all applicable local rules, including courtesy copy requirements.
